coredata.dc:title: Integration of carbon quantum dots with thermoplastic polyurethane for flexible and human-comfortable composite membrane
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Wei
Jing, Xiuchuan
Li, Zhuang
Miao, Haiqing
Wang, Zhong
coredata.dc:description: In this study, the carbon quantum dots/thermoplastic polyurethane (CQDs/TPU) flexible composite film is prepared by solvent and electrospinning methods. The effects of CQDs concentrations on the microstructure, mechanical property, breathable moisture-permeable property, and sensing performance of the composite films are investigated. Results show that the composite film prepared by solvent method has a large strain range, and its sensitivity increases with strain, but exhibits poor breathable moisture-permeable property and linearity. The fibers inside the electrospinning composite membrane are arranged in a staggered manner to form a good three-dimensional conductive network. As the concentration of CQDs increases, the tensile strength at break gradually decreases and the tensile strength at break gradually increases. The composite film has good breathability and moisture permeability, with a maximum air permeability of 2.44 L/(m2·s). The electrospinning method composite film has a smaller strain range but high sensitivity with a GF value of up to 53.3, better linearity, superior breathable moisture-permeable property and 4.67 times of air permeability compared to the composite film by solvent method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Flexible composite films
TPU
Conductive
Air permeability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterisation of CQDs/TPU flexible composite films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Nitrogen distribution and content on CQDs, (b) FTIR, (c) TEM, (d) diagram of TEM, (e) XRD, and (f) UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy.
TEM images for (a) TPU membrane and (b) electrospun fiber.
SEM images for (a-e) TPU membrane and (f-j) electrospun fiber in the upgrading order of CQDs concentration.
Tensile stress and elongation of CQDs/TPU composite film prepared by: (a) solvent method, and (b) electrospinning method.
The air and moisture permeability of CQDs/TPU composite films prepared by (a,c) solvent method, and (b,d) electrospinning method.
The volt vs. ampere characteristic curve of CQDs/TPU composite films prepared by (a) solvent method, and (b) electrospinning method.
The sensitivity vs. strain curve of CQDs/TPU composite films prepared by (a) solvent method, and (b) electrospinning method.
Parameter for fabrication of CQDs/TPU flexible composite film through solvent (CTAs) and electrospinning (CTBs) methods.
The sensitivity value of CQDs/TPU composite films prepared by solvent and electrospinning methods.

head.ce:title: Integration of carbon quantum dots with thermoplastic polyurethane for flexible and human-comfortable composite membrane

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Art, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Art, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Textile and Clothing Engineering, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Textile and Clothing Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Textile and Clothing Engineering, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the carbon quantum dots/thermoplastic polyurethane (CQDs/TPU) flexible composite film is prepared by solvent and electrospinning methods. The effects of CQDs concentrations on the microstructure, mechanical property, breathable moisture-permeable property, and sensing performance of the composite films are investigated. Results show that the composite film prepared by solvent method has a large strain range, and its sensitivity increases with strain, but exhibits poor breathable moisture-permeable property and linearity. The fibers inside the electrospinning composite membrane are arranged in a staggered manner to form a good three-dimensional conductive network. As the concentration of CQDs increases, the tensile strength at break gradually decreases and the tensile strength at break gradually increases. The composite film has good breathability and moisture permeability, with a maximum air permeability of 2.44 L/(m2·s). The electrospinning method composite film has a smaller strain range but high sensitivity with a GF value of up to 53.3, better linearity, superior breathable moisture-permeable property and 4.67 times of air permeability compared to the composite film by solvent method.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, the use of wearable electronic devices is expanding into a wider array of applications, which has led to an increasing need for flexible sensors. Nevertheless, traditional flexible sensors are often hindered by low sensitivity, a limited linear detection range, and the discomfort they can cause to users due to the inclusion of functional materials, which restricts their practical use. Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) have emerged as a promising nanomaterial, typically measuring <10 nm. These zero-dimensional carbon-based particles have garnered significant interest in recent years because of their distinctive physical and chemical properties [1–3]. Unlike conventional semiconductor quantum dots, such as CdSe and PbS, CQDs are primarily made of carbon and boast enhanced biocompatibility, reduced toxicity, and greater eco-friendliness. These attributes have positioned CQDs as a focal point in nanotechnology research [4]. CQDs consist of a carbon core surrounded by a variety of functional groups, including carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups, which impart water solubility and the potential for modification [5]. The synthesis of CQDs can be achieved through two main approaches, the top-down method, which involves breaking down larger carbon materials like graphite or carbon nanotubes into nano-sized particles via techniques such as laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, or chemical oxidation, and the bottom-up method, which uses biomass sources like citric acid or glucose, or organic molecules like urea, and employs processes such as hydrothermal, microwave, or pyrolysis methods [6,7].

Currently, there is a wide range of applications and advancements in integrating CQDs into composite materials. In the field of energy storage, CQDs are combined with electrodes, such as graphene or metal oxides, to boost conductivity and surface area, thereby enhancing the performance of supercapacitors and batteries [8,9]. Recent research has shown that these dots can significantly increase charge storage capacity and cycling stability [10,11]. Additionally, CQDs serve as photosensitizers when integrated with semiconductors like TiO₂ and g-C₃N₄, improving light absorption and electron transfer [12,13]. The focus is on designing heterojunctions to enhance efficiency in hydrogen production and pollutant degradation. Fluorescent CQD-polymer composites, such as those with polyvinyl alcohol, are used for detecting analytes through fluorescence quenching [14]. These innovations include real-time monitoring of heavy metals (Hg2+, Pb2+) and organic pollutants [15]. Biocompatible CQD composites are being explored for drug delivery, such as in hydrogel systems, and for bioimaging [16]. Studies highlight the importance of surface functionalization for targeted therapy and reduced toxicity. Magnetic CQD composites, like Fe₃O₄@CQDs, are effective for pollutant adsorption and magnetic recovery [17]. Photocatalytic membranes for water purification are also under development [18]. The current challenges include, addressing issues through surface functionalization (e.g., carboxyl groups) and compatible matrix materials, overcoming the transition from lab to industrial-scale synthesis, such as cost-effective and large-scale hydrothermal reactors, and conducting long-term toxicity assessments and ensuring regulatory compliance for biomedical applications.

Electrospinning CQDs into composite nanofibers presents unique challenges due to their nanoscale dimensions, surface chemistry, and interactions with polymer matrices. Firstly, CQDs tend to aggregate in polymer solutions due to high surface energy and van der Waals forces, leading to inhomogeneous fiber morphology [19]. To avoid this issue, modification of CQD surfaces with hydrophilic groups (e.g., –COOH, –NH₂) to enhance compatibility with polar solvents/polymers [20], using solvents that stabilize CQD dispersion (e.g., DMF, ethanol-water mixtures) [21] and prolonging sonication or magnetic stirring to break agglomerates before electrospinning [22], are three commonly used solution. Moreover, adding CQDs to polymer solution to alter its viscosity and conductivity, which destabilizes the Taylor cone (charged jet) and causes bead formation or irregular fibers, which could be tackled with low-concentration polymers (e.g., PVA, PVP) to balance viscosity, with ionic salts (e.g., NaCl) to improve charge transfer while maintaining spinnability, and to adjust voltage (10–30 kV) and flow rate (0.1–2 mL/h) to stabilize the electrospinning jet. In addition, weak interactions between CQDs and the polymer matrix can lead to phase separation or leaching of CQDs [23]. Such issue is solvable by functionalizing CQDs with reactive groups (e.g., epoxy, acrylate) to form bonds with the polymer; Introduce crosslinkers (e.g., glutaraldehyde) to strengthen the CQD-polymer network; High-voltage electrospinning and solvent evaporation may degrade CQDs, etc.

Thus in this study, CQDs are applied as conductive fillers and TPU (thermoplastic polyurethane) commonly used for flexible substrates [24], is adopted to prepare two types of CQDs/TPU flexible composite films through solvent and electrospinning methods. CQDs are prepared using citric acid as the carbon source and urea as the nitrogen-containing doping material. The experimental content includes observing the microstructure of the composite film through scanning electron microscopy and transmission electron microscopy, testing the breathability and permeability of the composite film using a fully automatic breathability meter and a fabric breathability meter, and testing the mechanical and sensing properties of the composite film using a universal material testing machine and an electrochemical workstation. By comparing their differences in microstructure, mechanical properties, breathability, permeability, and sensing performance, this study explores the effects of different preparation methods and the concentration of CQDs on the properties of composite membranes.

TPU (90 A, density: 1.02 g/cm3, original colour) was purchased from Shanghai J&K Scientific, Co., Ltd., China. N. N-dimethylformamide (DMF, AR grade) was obtained from Jiangsu Argon Krypton Xenon Material Technology Co., Ltd., China. Sodium dodecylbenzenesulfonate (SDBS, AR grade) was bought from Shanghai Aladdin Co., Ltd., China. Citric acid (CA, AR grade) and Urea (AR grade) are obtained from Youtep Technology Suzhou Co., Ltd., China.

Citric acid (0.97 g, 5.0 mmol) and urea (0.90 g, 15.0 mmol) were dissolved them in 15 mL of deionized water. The mixture was subject to ultra-sonication for 10 min, then transferred to the PTFE reaction vessel, and placed in a vacuum oven to heat at 180 °C for 6 h. After the complete of reaction, the mixture was cooled down naturally to room temperature and then subsequently centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove larger particles. The supernatant was taken and filtered by a dialysis bag in deionized water at room temperature for 12 h with water renewing every 3 h for 4 sessions in total. The resultant was dried at 80 °C to obtain yellow solid powder of CQDs, and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. A brief illustration is described in Fig. 1 .

The CQDs/TPU flexible composite film was prepared through solvent method according to Table 1 . The TPU masterbatch was dried in an oven to remove moisture, then added to 20 mL DMF solution, and stirred continuously for 8 h (Sample 1). In order to evenly disperse CQDs in DMF, the SDBS was grounded with CQDs thoroughly. Then, the mixed powder was added to 10 mL DMF solution and stirred continuously for 8 h (Sample 2). Sample 1 and 2 are mixed completely, stirred continuously for 4 h, poured into a Teflon mold (10 cm × 10 cm × 2 cm) and placed in a well ventilated environment to evaporate the solvent completely at around 25 °C with the relative humidity between 40 % and 60 % to avoid cracks or deformation occurred on the surface of the flexible composite film.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Preparation of electrospinning solution: Sample 1 and 2 were mixed completely in a volume ratio of 2:1 in the solvent to prepare the electrospinning solution. The electrospinning solution was then place on a magnetic stirrer and stirred continuously for 12 h for subsequent usage.

Electrospinning process: A syringe was adopted to extract the spinning solution with a needle size of 20 G with an inner diameter of 0.64 mm. The spinning voltage, temperature, relative humidity, injection pump speed, receiving distance and receiving roller speed were set to 15 kV, 25 °C, 40 %, 0.5 mL/h, 15 cm, and 400 r/min, respectively. The receiving device was wrapped with silicone oil paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The EDS spectroscopies on CQDs were carried out on a desktop scanning electron microscope (TSEM, TM3030, Hitachi, Japan) to explore the elemental composition and distribution within the sample. To prepare the sample, CQDs powder was evenly spread onto an electron microscope stage adhered with a conductive adhesive using a glass rod, and remove the excessive powders and impurities by compress air. An infrared lamp for 5 min irradiation was applied to remove moisture from the sample, and then finally was spray coated with gold. During the observation by TSEM, the acceleration voltage of SEM was set as 15 kV with the current of 5 mA. The morphology of CQDs/TPU was observed by cold field scanning electronic microscope (S-4700, Hitachi, Japan).

The functional groups and chemical bonds in CQDs samples were identified by FTIR (NICOLET5700is5, Thermal Fisher, USA). The CQDs sample was mixed with potassium bromide in a ratio of 1:100, and ground thoroughly with a mortar, and dried with infrared lamp. The mixed powder was then pressed into a transparent thin tablet before placing into the testing chamber. The wavenumber range was set as 4000–400 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1 and a scanning frequency of 16 times.

The microstructures of CQDs and electrospun samples were observed through transmission electron microscopy (TEM HT 7700, Hitachi, Japan) under vacuum condition at 100 kV. To prepare the sample, CQDs dispersion was diluted with ionized water by 50 times, transferred onto a 200 mesh copper mesh using a pipette, and dried at 60 °C.

The crystal structure of CQDs was studied using X-ray diffractometer (X'Pert-Pro MPD diffractometer, PANalytical B.V., Almelo, Netherlands) using Cu-Ka radiation of wavelength 0.15418 nm at an angle range of 10°–40°, scanning speed of 4°/min.. For sample preparation, the CQDs powder was ground in a mortar, and spread it flat in a sample tray.

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CQDs was taken on a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Zeiss, Germany) at a wavelength range of 200–600 nm with a resolution of 2 nm. Before test, the sample solution was diluted 20 times to ensure the suitable absorbance between 0.1 and 1.0.
The tensile property of CQDs/TPU was measured on Instro5967 tensile machine (INSTRON, Co., USA). The sample was cut into 40 mm × 10 mm with a clamp distance of 20 mm at a pulling speed of 10 mm/min.

The air permeability was measured on YG461G automatic air permeability meter (Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd., China) to test the breathability performance of CQDs/TPU composite films. The sample film was into a circular shape with a diameter of 6 cm. The pressure and nozzle diameter were set as 100 Pa, and 0.8 mm, respectively. The measurement was implemented for three times for an average result to be displayed.

The moisture permeability was evaluated on FX3150 automatic fabric moisture permeability tester (Hitachi, Co., Ltd., Japan) using the cup upward method. The sample film was cut into a round shape in a diameter of 6 cm. The temperature was set respectively to 25 °C, 30 °C, and 35 °C at relative humidity of 50 %. The weigh after each 30 min was recorded.

A CHI760E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd., China) was used to test the electrical properties of CQDs/TPU flexible composite films. Firstly, composite film was cut into a rectangle shape in 10 mm × 40 mm, fixed by a fixture to maintain a flat with stress. Secondly, both ends were connected to the electrochemical workstation to test and record the data of the current variation of the composite film with voltage. The current testing method adopts cyclic voltammetry, with a working voltage of -5 V–5 V, with an interval time of 0.1 s, and a sensitivity of 0.01. To evaluate the sensitivity of CQDs/TPU flexible composite films, a Universal Material Experimenter was applied accompanied with the electrochemical workstation at a pulling speed of 10 mm/min with a clamp distance of 20 mm.

The elemental composition and distribution on the surface of CQDs were analyzed using EDS spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 2a. The elemental composition of CQDs is mainly composed of C, N and O. The distribution of N on the surface of CQDs is relatively uniform without obvious aggregation, indicating the effective doping of N in CQDs. In order to further understand the functional groups of CQDs, infrared spectroscopy tests were conducted. According to the analysis of Fig. 2b, the broad peaks at 3230 cm−1, 1720 cm−1, 1640 cm−1 and 1410 cm−1 in the infrared spectrum are the stretching vibration of O-H/N-H bond, CO bond, CC bond, and CN bond, respectively [25]. The results above confirm that the surface of CQDs may contain –NH, –OH, and –COOH groups, which provide CQDs with enhanced water solubility and dispersibility [26]. And also the N atoms are successfully doped onto CQDs.

As shown in Fig. 2c,d, the morphology and size of CQDs were observed by TEM. The morphology of CQDs is elliptical spherical nanoparticle. It is also observed that there are few CQDs aggregates formed, showing a certain degree of dispersion performance. According to the analysis of the electron microscopy image, it is found that the average particle size of CQDs was 4.62 ± 0.05 nm, and which displays normal distribution (See the figure insert), indicating the uniform of the particle size of CQDs.

The diffraction pattern of CQDs characterized by XRD is shown in Fig. 2e. A broad diffraction peak appears at the center of 2θ = 25° in the diffraction pattern, which corresponds to the (002) crystal plane structure of graphene [27]. This indicates that the prepared CQDs have typical characteristics of graphite carbon materials, and there is a distribution of SP2 structured graphene crystals on the carbon skeleton of CQDs Synthesis and Application of Fluorescent N, S Co-Doped Carbon Dots Based on On-Off-On Quenching Mode for The Collaboration Detection of Iron Ions And Ascorbic Acid [28].

Fig. 2f shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CQDs, in which the two broad absorption peaks at 228 nm and 342 nm, correspond to the π–π * transition of the conjugated CC bond and the n–π* transition of the conjugated CO bond in CQDs, respectively [29]. The UV absorption feature of CQDs is derived from the presence of conjugated double bonds, which are formed by dehydration reactions between CA molecules [30]. The photo shows that the CQDs solution display a shallow yellowish appearance under sunlight, and emits blue fluorescence when exposed to 365 nm UV light, thus indicating the photoluminescence effect of the fabricated CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterisation of CQDs/TPU flexible composite films

ce:section.ce:para: Seen from the TEM image in Fig. 3a, the CQDs are dispersed unevenly within the TPU composite film prepared by solvent method. From the magnified TEM image, it is observed that CQDs aggregate and form larger particles within the composite film. Fig. 3b shows the microstructure of fibers through electrospinning. The electrospun fibers are in nanoscale and relatively uniform in diameter. The fiber surface is smooth without the formation of aggregates. Seen from the magnified TEM image, it is found that CQDs are uniformly wrapped within the fibers by TPU. The reason for the difference in the dispersion of CQDs between in the TPU membrane and electrospun fiber is mainly related to the solvent evaporation rate. The solvent evaporation time of the solution composite membrane is longer, thus inducing the agglomeration of CQDs during the evaporation process due to the surface tension of the solution. Under the stretching effect of high-voltage electric field force, electrospun fibers have a short curing time, making it difficult for the agglomeration of CQDs in TPU thus making uniform dispersion.

As shown in Fig. 4 (a-e), the SEM images show that the surface of the TPU composite film prepared by solvent method gradually becomes rough from smooth. The reason for this phenomenon is that when the DMF solvent evaporates, and TPU wrapped around CQDs form aggregates on the surface of the composite film. The more pronounced the roughness of the composite membrane surface is observed at higher concentration of CQDs. Seen from Fig. 4(f-j), the electrospun fibers are arranged in a staggered manner to form a network structure. The surface of the fibers is smooth without the attachment of aggregates, indicating that CQDs are wrapped inside the fibers by TPU. As the amount of CQDs increases, the diameter of the fibers and the adhesion tendency between the fibers gradually increases. When the concentration of CQDs is below 7.5 %, there is no adhesion occurred between fibers. The diameter of fiber is small but in a large difference. When the concentration of CQDs reaches 10 %, the fiber diameter becomes larger but the difference gets smaller. When the concentration of CQDs is >12.5 %, the adhesion phenomenon of fibers is very obvious. This is because the larger the amount of CQDs, the higher the viscosity of the spinning solvent, and excessively high viscosity is not conducive to stretching and refinement of fiber.

Fig. 5a shows the stress-strain curves of the CQDs/TPU composite film prepared by solvent method. As the concentration of CQDs increases, the tensile stress and elongation of the CQDs/TPU composite film at break gradually decrease. When the concentration of CQDs reaches 5.0 %, the maximum elongation of the composite film is 848.3 %, and the tensile stress is 51.2 MPa. When the concentration of CQDs is 15.0 %, the fracture tensile strength of the composite film is 399.8 %, and the fracture stress was 19.7 MPa. The fracture elongation and fracture stress decreased by 52.9 % and 61.5 %, respectively. This is because the particle size and aspect ratio of CQDs are small, which induces their agglomeration within the composite film. As the concentration of CQDs increases, the stress concentration points caused by agglomeration increase, resulting in a decrease in the fracture stress and fracture tensile strength of the composite film. Fig. 5b shows the stress-strain curve of CQDs/TPU composite film produced by electrospinning method. The fracture tensile strength of the composite film gradually decreases with the increase of CQDs concentration, while the fracture strength gradually increases. Among them, when the concentration of CQDs increased from 5.0 % to 15.0 %, the fracture tensile rate of the composite film decreased from 265.0 % to 195.4 %, and the corresponding fracture stress increased from 4.86 MPa to 7.64 MPa. The reason is that when the concentration of CQDs is low, the fiber diameter is thinner and the degree of adhesion is low. When stretched, the fibers are prone to slip, so the composite film is easily stretched and deformed, and has a higher elongation at break. As the concentration of CQDs increases, the fibers of the composite membrane gradually become thicker and the degree of adhesion increases, and the modulus of the fibers increases. Therefore, the fracture stress of the composite membrane also gradually increases.

In all, the composite film prepared by solvent method is better in mechanical property than the one prepared by electrospinning method. The possible reason is that the TPU substrate in the solvent method is integrity with a dense internal structure, thus having strong mechanical properties. However, the electrospinning composite membrane has a small fiber diameter, poor orientation, and weak contact between fibers, resulting in poor mechanical properties of the composite membrane.

The flexible composite film used for human signal detection closely contact with the skin, thus its breathability and moisture permeability are particularly important, which determines the comfort of the users when wearing it. Seen from Fig. 6a, the air permeability of the composite membrane prepared by solvent method gradually increases with the increase of CQDs concentration. When the concentration of CQDs rises from 5.0 % to 15.0 %, the air permeability of the composite membrane increases from 0.45 L/(m2·s) to 0.52 L/(m2·s), respectively. In general, the air permeability is relatively poor and shows marginal change with the concentration of CQDs, and the air permeability. From Fig. 6b, the electrospun composite membrane gradually decreases with the increase of CQDs concentration. When the concentration of CQDs is 5.0 %, the air permeability of the membrane reaches 2.44 L/(m2·s), which is 4.67 times over the maximum air permeability of the composite membrane prepared by solvent method.

The permeability of composite membranes is mainly related to the fiber diameter and pore distribution of the membrane. Comparing the composite films prepared by two different methods, the air permeability of the electrospinning composite film is superior to that of the solution composite film. From the TEM image, the surface structure of the composite membrane by solvent method is rough with fewer pores, resulting in poor air permeability. For comparison, the electrospun composite membranes have a porous structure and finer fiber diameter, resulting in better air permeability.

Fig. 6c, d show the moisture permeability of CQDs/TPU composite films under different testing conditions. As shown in the figure, with the increase of CQDs concentration, the moisture permeability of the solution method composite membrane gradually increases, while the moisture permeability of the electrospinning method composite membrane gradually decreases. Moreover, with the increase of testing temperature, the moisture permeability of both composite membranes gradually increases. The threshold for the moisture permeability of textiles is 3000 g/(m2·d) according to the standard GB/T 40910-2021 for the evaluation of waterproof and moisture permeable performance of textiles. The maximum moisture permeability of solution based composite membranes is only 689 g/(m2·d), while the moisture permeability of electrospun composite membranes at CQDs concentrations of 5.0 %, 7.5 %, and 10.0 % are 3851 g/(m2·d), 3611 g/(m2·d), and 3099 g/(m2·d) at 25 °C, respectively. Therefore, the moisture permeability of electrospun composite membranes is superior to that of solution composite membranes, making them more suitable for fields such as flexible wearable devices or electronic skin.

The volt vs. ampere characteristic curve of the composite film is plot according to the change in current along with voltage of the composite film without applied force. From Fig. 7a, the volt vs. ampere characteristic curves of all composite membranes go through the origin. The current shows a linear relationship with voltage, indicating that the solvent method and electrospinning method composite membranes have good “Ohmic” characteristics within the voltage range. As the concentration of CQDs increases, the slope of the current gradually increases, indicating that the resistance of the composite film is gradually decreasing. Comparing the current slopes of two types of composite films, it is found that the electrical properties of the electrospun composite film are better than the one prepared by solvent method at the same concentration. This is because the electrospun composite film has a good three-dimensional conductive network and multiple internal conductive pathways, resulting in better conductivity.

The sensitivity of a sensor is used to measure its response to the strain of material, which is usually applied to evaluate the detection performance of the sensor under deformation. In strain sensors, sensitivity is often expressed as the relationship between resistance change and unit strain as the following equation,
GF =
ΔR
R 0
ε

Among them, GF is sensitivity, ΔR is resistance change, R
0 is initial resistance, and ε is the unit change of strain. The conductive network of CQDs/TPU flexible composite film is formed by CQDs wrapped inside. The working mechanism of the composite film is to change the internal conductive path through external force, resulting in a change in the material's resistance.

As shown in Fig. 8a, the strain sensitivity curve of the solution method composite film exhibits a three-stage distribution, which is divided into low strain zone S1 (0–30 %), medium strain zone S2 (30%–60 %), and high strain zone S3 (60%–90 %). The resistance change rate of the composite film gradually increases with the increase of strain. In the S1 (<30 %) region, the composite film is subjected to stretching, and the distance between the conductive fillers CQDs begins to change, causing some damage to the internal conductive pathways. However, due to the small deformation, the change in resistance is not significant, resulting in lower sensitivity. In the S2 (30%–60 %) region, the composite film continues to be stretched, the distance between CQDs increases, the resistance change rate begins to rapidly increase, and the sensitivity also increases rapidly. In the S3 (60%–90 %) region, the internal conductive pathways of the composite film are almost completely destroyed, the resistance change rate begins to increase sharply, and the internal conductive pathways gradually become disconnected. At this point, the sensitivity of the composite film reaches its maximum. In addition, in the S3 region, as the concentration of CQDs increases, the maximum response strains of the composite membrane are 70.0 %, 73.8 %, 78.3 %, 81.4 %, and 87.5 %, respectively. The strain response range of the solution method composite membrane in the S3 region gradually decreases with the increase of CQDs concentration. According to Table 2 , when the concentration of CQDs is 5.0 %, the sensitivity of the composite membrane is the highest, with sensitivities of 3.8, 11.6, and 41.3 in the S1, S2, and S3 regions, respectively. When the concentration of CQDs is 15.0 %, the sensitivity of the composite membrane is the lowest, with sensitivities of 2.0, 5.7, and 28.3 in the S1, S2, and S3 regions, respectively. The sensitivity of the solution method composite membrane gradually decreases with the increase of CQDs concentration and gradually increases with the increase of strain.

The sensitivity curve of the electrospun composite film is analyzed and shown in Fig. 8b. The increase of strain, the sensitivity of the electrospun composite film shows a two-stage change. The strain response range is divided into a low strain zone G1 (<20 %) and a high strain zone G2 (20%–50 %), and the higher the strain, the higher the sensitivity, which is similar to the strain sensors previously reported [31]. Due to the unique three-dimensional network structure and multiple internal conductive pathways of electrospun composite films, the resistance changes of the composite film are sensitive to strain, and the relative range of resistance changes is large. When the composite film is stretched, the conductive network inside the composite film is damaged and its resistance and sensitivity rapidly increase. As the strain continues to increase, the stretching of polyurethane fibers leads to an increase in the distance between conductive fillers, causing a sharp change in tunneling resistance and a sharp increase in the sensitivity of the composite film. As the strain continues to increase, the internal contact of the composite film has become a whole, and the circuit state is close to breaking, but weak current is still detected and the change is not significant. At this point, the strain reaches the strain response range of the composite film, and the sensitivity no longer increases. The sensitivity of electrospun composite films gradually decreases with the increase of CQDs concentration, because the closer the concentration of conductive fillers in the composite film is to the percolation concentration, the greater the resistance change of the composite film to strain, that is, the higher the sensitivity. When the concentration of CQDs is 5.0 %, the sensitivity of the composite membrane is the highest, with G1 (<20 %) and G2 (20%–50 %) showing sensitivities of 20.2 and 53.3, respectively. When the concentration of CQDs reaches a maximum of 15.0 %, the sensitivity of the composite membrane in G1 (<20 %) and G2 (20%–50 %) is 4.4 and 12.6, respectively. The sensitivity of electrospun composite films also gradually increases with the increase of strain, and the sensitivity of electrospun composite films is higher under lower concentration conditions, with a strain response range of 0–50 %.

Comparing the composite films prepared by two methods, the solution method has a wide range of strain, and the higher the strain, the higher the sensitivity. It is insensitive to small deformations and has poor linearity. The strain range of electrospinning composite film is relatively small, but it has high sensitivity and good linearity of the response curve.

In this study, composite membranes with different concentrations of CQDs were prepared using solvent and electrospinning methods. The mechanical property, air and moisture permeability, and sensing performance are compared. Results show that the surface of the composite membrane prepared by solvent method is rough, and the distribution of CQDs inside is uneven, and the breathability and moisture permeability are poor. The composite film has excellent mechanical properties. When the concentration of CQDs is 5.0 %, the fracture tensile rate of the composite film is the highest, reaching 848.3 %, and the maximum fracture stress is 51.2 MPa. The sensitivity of the composite film gradually decreases with the increase of CQDs concentration, and the higher the strain, the higher the sensitivity. When the concentration of CQDs is 5.0 %, the sensitivity of the composite membrane is the highest. The sensitivity of the composite membrane is 3.8 within the strain range of 0–30 %, 11.4 within the strain range of 30 % ~ 60 %, and 41.3 within the strain range of 60–70 %. The fibers inside the electrospinning composite membrane are arranged in a staggered manner to form a good three-dimensional conductive network, but the mechanical properties are poor. As the concentration of CQDs increases, the tensile strength at break gradually decreases and the tensile strength at break gradually increases. The composite film has good breathability and moisture permeability, with a maximum air permeability of 2.44 L/(m2·s). The lower the concentration of CQDs, the greater the sensitivity of the composite membrane. Within the two methods, the composite film fabricated by solvent method has better mechanical property, a wider strain response range, but poorer breathability and moisture permeability, and lower sensitivity to small deformations compared with the electrospinning composite films, thus making them more suitable for the preparation of flexible electronic devices.

Wei Chen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xiuchuan Jing: Software, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Zhuang Li: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Haiqing Miao: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zhong Wang: Supervision, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the Young Elite Scientists Sponsorship Program by JSAST (JSTJ-2023-XH056).
